topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The postcard was tucked beneath a stack of forgotten textbooks, the vibrant image of a distant coastline mocking the muted grey of my current reality. When I finally unfolded it, the neat, unfamiliar address swam before my eyes. Liam. Moving. Across the country. A heavy, dull ache settled in my chest, a familiar pressure behind my ribs that made breathing feel like a conscious effort. I traced the outline of his name with a shaky finger, the paper crinkling under the pressure. I didn't move to put it away; I just stared at it, the world around me seeming to slow down to a sluggish crawl. Every sound in the room—the hum of the refrigerator, the distant traffic—felt amplified and irritatingly loud. I picked up my phone, the screen illuminating my face in a pale, unflattering light, and stared blankly at the contact labeled 'Liam.' The silence in my apartment was vast, echoing the emptiness that had suddenly expanded within me.

[story 2]

I was meticulously cleaning my desk, a pointless exercise in order when the envelope arrived. It was Liam’s handwriting, crisp and decisive, detailing the logistics of his departure. Reading the details felt like watching a slow-motion train leave the station. My hands, usually steady enough for intricate tasks, began to tremble, causing the pen to skip across the page. I dropped it onto the worn wood. A profound weariness washed over me, a deep-seated fatigue that rooted me to the floor. I leaned my forehead against the cool surface of the desk, closing my eyes tightly. The air felt thick, unbreathable. It wasn't just sadness; it was a complete system shutdown. I could feel the weight of the impending distance pressing down on my shoulders, making them feel impossibly heavy, like stones I couldn't lift.

[story 3]

The news hit me during a mundane Tuesday afternoon, while I was waiting for coffee. Liam had confirmed it—a move, a new life thousands of miles away. For a full minute, nothing happened. I simply stood there, the warmth of the mug suddenly feeling cold against my palms. My gaze drifted past the cafe window, focusing on a passing car, trying to anchor myself to something tangible. The effort of maintaining composure was monumental. My shoulders slumped involuntarily, a visible surrender to the enormity of the change. I took a slow, shallow breath, and the effort of drawing air seemed to cost me everything. My limbs felt heavy, weighted with an invisible sediment. I managed a weak, almost inaudible murmur, "Oh." And then I just stood there, letting the quiet, overwhelming sense of displacement settle deep within my core.
